title: Wet-felted sleep mask
subtitle: Two layers of merino over a flat oval resist, finished to roughly 20 by 8 cm
excerpt: Wet-felt a sleep mask from fine merino roving using a simple flat oval resist. The two-layer layout gives the mask enough body to hold its shape; shrinkage allowance of around 25 per cent means you start with a resist closer to 27 by 11 cm. A good project for migraine ease or cool sleep.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from the general wet-felting tradition. The cross-hatch two-layer layout and the flat-oval resist method are standard workshop practice, observation-led rather than book-cited.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre drawn into a long ribbon ready for spinning or felting. The staples lie roughly parallel along the ribbon's length, making it easy to pull thin layers from the end.

The stage of wet felting that follows initial bonding: the pre-felt is rinsed, then agitated more vigorously (thrown against a hard surface, rolled tightly, or rubbed briskly) to compact the fibres fully and shrink the piece to its finished size.

Wool that has been wet down and worked lightly enough that the fibres have begun to lock but the sheet can still be cut or shaped without fraying. Pre-felt holds its form while the resist is adjusted or removed.

A flat template placed inside a layered wool layout to prevent the two faces from felting together, creating a pocket or hollow. In flat-panel pieces the resist defines the outline shape; removing it before final fulling allows the opening to be worked closed or left as an edge.

content.text: A wet-felted sleep mask starts with two thin layers of fine merino
roving
laid over a flat oval
resist

cut from dense foam or stiff plastic. The wet-felting process shrinks the layout by roughly 25 per cent in every direction, so the resist needs to start at approximately 27 by 11 cm to produce a finished mask of around 20 by 8 cm. Fine merino at 17 to 19 microns gives the smoothest skin contact; coarser breeds are not suitable for anything worn next to the face.
What you need

items.name: Olive-oil soap bar or mild wool wash
Hot water (as hot as your hands can bear)
Dense foam sheet or stiff plastic folder (for the resist)
Cotton tea towel or bubble wrap (for rolling surface)
Felting net or fine mesh (optional, holds surface layers in place)
1.5 cm-wide elastic or ribbon (for mask strap)
Blunt darning needle and strong thread (to attach strap)

content.text: Cut the resist from a dense foam sheet (a yoga mat off-cut works) or a stiff plastic folder. Trace an oval approximately 27 cm long and 11 cm wide at the centre, with a slight inward curve at the nose bridge. Cut cleanly. This shape will shrink with the felt and be removed mid-way through
fulling
Method
Step 1: Lay out the first face

Lay the resist flat on the bamboo mat. Pull thin wisps of merino from the roving and lay them in a single horizontal layer across the resist, extending about 2 cm beyond the resist edge all the way around. Pull the wisps evenly thin; thick spots will be visible in the finished piece and will felt unevenly. When the resist is covered horizontally, lay a second layer of wisps running vertically, crossing the first at 90 degrees. This cross-hatch layout ensures the fibres lock together in both directions. The two layers together should weigh about 10 g.
Step 2: Wet down the first face

Dissolve a pea-sized amount of olive-oil soap in hot water, or use a spray bottle with a few drops of wool wash. Wet the layout from the centre outward, pressing gently to saturate each layer without disturbing the arrangement. If you have a felting net, lay it over the surface before wetting; it holds the wisps in place. Rub lightly through the net for 3 to 4 minutes in circular motions until the surface starts to hold together. Remove the net carefully.
Step 3: Turn and lay the second face

Carefully lift the mat, flip it so the wet layout is now face-down on a fresh dry surface, and fold the overhanging edges of the wet fibre back over the resist. These folded edges will lock both faces together around the perimeter. Lay another two cross-hatched layers of roving on this new exposed face of the resist, matching the weight of the first side. Wet down and work through the net as before for another 3 to 4 minutes.
Step 4: Rub to pre-felt stage

Continue working both faces alternately, flipping every 3 to 4 minutes, keeping the surface hot and soapy. After 15 to 20 minutes total the fibres should have begun to bond and the layout will hold together without the net. This is the
pre-felt

stage: the piece can be peeled back from the resist without falling apart. Pinch a corner between thumb and finger; if the fibres pull free as individual strands, continue rubbing. When the surface holds as a coherent sheet, you are ready to remove the resist.
Step 5: Remove the resist and full to size

Cut a small slit along one long edge of the mask, through the felt only, and ease the resist out. Squeeze the opening closed with your fingers and work it back into the surface by rubbing. Now begin fulling: roll the mask tightly inside the bamboo mat and roll back and forth on the worktop with firm hand pressure for 3 to 4 minutes, then unroll, rotate the piece 90 degrees, and roll again. Alternate rolling direction every few minutes. Periodically throw the piece firmly onto the worktop surface 10 to 15 times to shock the fibres further. Continue until the mask has reached approximately 20 by 8 cm and feels firm and dense throughout; this typically takes 20 to 30 minutes of active working.
Step 6: Rinse and block

Rinse the mask thoroughly in hot water to remove all soap, then plunge it into cold water for 30 seconds to shock the fibres closed. Squeeze gently; do not wring. Reshape by hand to the oval form, pulling the nose-bridge curve back if it has rounded out. Lay flat on a dry towel and leave to dry away from direct heat. Once dry, trim the perimeter with sharp scissors to a clean even edge if any loose wisps have frayed at the margin.
Step 7: Attach the strap

Thread a 40 cm length of 1.5 cm elastic or ribbon through a blunt darning needle and sew the ends to the inner side of the mask at each temple point, roughly 2 cm from the edge. Work several passes through the felt, then knot securely. Adjust the length before cutting if a different fit is needed.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The mask shrank more on one side than the other
items.cause: The roving layers were thicker on one side, or rubbing pressure was uneven across the surface
items.fix: Lay the next piece in thinner, more even wisps and check thickness by holding it up to a light before wetting. Apply equal pressure across the full surface while rubbing, not just the centre.

items.problem: The two faces did not bond at the edges
items.cause: The folded-over overhang was too short, or the edges were not worked sufficiently
items.fix: Leave at least 2 cm of overhang beyond the resist on every side. After folding the overhang back, rub those edges specifically for an extra minute before moving to the centre.

items.problem: The resist is difficult to remove without tearing the pre-felt
items.cause: The felt was not worked long enough before attempting removal, or the resist is too rigid
items.fix: Continue rubbing until the pinch test holds before cutting the slit. A flexible foam resist is easier to extract than rigid plastic because it can be rolled up inside the felt pocket.

items.problem: The finished mask is too stiff against the face
items.cause: Over-fulling, or the roving was too coarse for face wear
items.fix: Check the micron count of the roving before buying; 17 to 19 microns is the maximum for comfortable face contact. Reduce fulling time slightly and test the hand-feel before the final rinse.

content.text: Care and finishing

Hand-wash in cool water with a few drops of wool wash. Do not rub; press and squeeze gently. Rinse until the water runs clear. Roll in a towel to remove excess water, reshape, and dry flat. Machine washing will shrink the piece further and cause uneven distortion. Store flat or rolled loosely. Steam and reshape lightly if the mask loses its curve after repeated wear.
